concepts.title: Motional EMF
concepts.conceptBody:
Motional Emf

If a conductor moves in a magnetic field then an emf is induced in the conductor across its ends.

Consider a rod of length 1 moving in the $\vec{B}$ field. Hence, electrons experience magnetic force.

Due to accumulation of charge at one end, $\vec{E}$ field is set up inside the conductor and at any given instant the $\vec{E}$ force becomes equal to $\vec{B}$ force, when further motion of the electrons stop.

${{\overrightarrow{\text{F}}}_{\text{B}}}\,\,+\,\,{{\overrightarrow{\text{F}}}_{\text{e}}}\,\,=\,\,0$

$\Rightarrow [-\text{e}(\overrightarrow{\text{v}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{B}})]+\,\,-\text{e}\overrightarrow{\text{E}}\,\,=\,\,0$

$\,\,\,\,\,\therefore,$Now, the potential drop that exists along the ends MN of the conductor is given by

$\left( {{V}_{M}}-{{V}_{N}} \right)=-\int{{\vec{E}}}\cdot \overrightarrow{dl}$

` $\therefore \,\,\,{{\text{V}}_{\text{MN}}}\,\,=\,\,\int{\overrightarrow{\text{B}}}\cdot \,\,\,(\overrightarrow{\text{dl}}\times \overrightarrow{\text{v}})$

The direction of induced emf can be obtained using the Fleming's Right Hand Rule.

Fleming's Right Hand Rule

index $\to $ along the $\vec{B}$ field

thumb $\to $ along the velocity

middle finger $\to $ direction of induced emf

In general, to solve the question based on motional emf.

1.Resolve the $\vec{B}$ field into two components, one parallel to the plane of motion and the other perpendicular to the plane of motion.

(No contribution of the component of $\vec{B}$ parallel to the field)

2.Resolve the velocity vector $\vec{v}$ in the plane of motion of the rod into two components, one along the length of the rod and one perpendicular to the length of the rod.

(No contribution of the component of $\vec{v}$ parallel to the rod).

3.Apply \[\Delta V\text{ }\,=\,\,\int{{{B}_{\bot }}}\cdot {{\overrightarrow{\text{v}}}_{\bot }}d\text{l}\]

Note : If $\vec{B},\vec{v}$ are mutually perpendicular

$\therefore \quad \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,Emf\,\,induced\,\,\,=\,\,\,\int{\overrightarrow{B}}\cdot (\overrightarrow{dl}\times \overrightarrow{v})$

\[=\,\,\overrightarrow{B}\cdot \left( \int{\overrightarrow{dl}}\times \overrightarrow{v} \right)\]

$=\,\,\overrightarrow{B}\cdot \left( {{\overrightarrow{l}}_{eff}}\times \overrightarrow{v} \right)$

$\therefore \quad \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,$ $only\,when\,\,\overrightarrow{B}\,\,is\,perpendicular\,to\,\overrightarrow{v}$

$\therefore \quad \,For\,closed\,loop,\,\,\oint{\overrightarrow{dl}}=\,\,0,\,\,\,\,\therefore \,\,emf\,induced\,\,=\,\,0$

Note that, when $\vec{B}$ and $\vec{v}$ are perpendicular and we have a closed loop then net emf induced in the entire loop is zero, (but not zero for individual segments).

concepts.title: Motional Emf Induced Along Rod
concepts.conceptBody:
Motional Emf Induced Along Rod

Consider a rod of length 1 which is rotated in uniform field B as shown.

 

emf induced$=\int_{0}^{1}{B}(\omega x)dx$

$\left( {{\text{V}}_{0}}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{A}}} \right)=\frac{\text{B}\omega {{\text{l}}^{2}}}{2}\quad (direction\,\,obtained\,\,from\,\,Flemin{g}'s\,\,Right\,\,Hand\,\,rule)$

Note that an emf is induced even in the absence of a magnetic field when a conductor is rotated

about an axis because of the centrifugal force experienced by each particle.

But the net situation after considering the effect of centrifugal force can be described as :

$\text{eE}\,\,=\,\,\text{m}{{\omega }^{2}}\text{x}\,\,+\,\,\text{eB}\,\,(\omega x)$

$\therefore \quad \,\,\,\,\,\,\text{E}=\,\,\left( \frac{\text{m}}{\text{e}} \right){{\omega }^{2}}\text{x}+\,\,(\text{B}\omega \text{x})$

 

∴ the actual potential drop across the ends is given as

$\left( {{\text{V}}_{0}}-{{\text{V}}_{\text{M}}} \right)\,\,=\,\,-\int_{{{\text{r}}_{\text{m}}}}^{{{\text{r}}_{0}}}{\overrightarrow{\text{E}}}\cdot d\overrightarrow{x}\,\,\,=\,\,-\int_{\text{l}}^{0}{E}dx$

$=\int_{0}^{1}{\left( \frac{\text{m}}{\text{e}}{{\omega }^{2}}\text{x}+\,\,\text{B}\omega \text{x} \right)}\,\text{dx}\,\,=\left[ \text{B}\omega \,\,\,+\,\,\frac{\text{m}{{\omega }^{2}}}{\text{e}} \right]\,\,\frac{{{1}^{2}}}{2}$

But, since $\frac{m}{e}$ is much smaller, the quantity $\frac{m{{\omega }^{2}}\,\,{{l}^{2}}}{2e}$ is neglected and hence we derive the particular result.

concepts.title: EMF Induced of Rotating Conductor in Magnetic Field
concepts.conceptBody:
Emf Induced of Rotating Conductor in Magnetic Field 

  • Note that the $\overrightarrow{dl}$ vector will be directed to the tangent direction of the shape of conductor at that point
  • The velocity vector $(\omega r)$ is directed perpendicular to the $\vec{r}$ vector

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$Let the angle between them be $\theta $.

∴ emf induced in this small element

$\begin{align} & =\text{Bdl}(\omega \text{r}\sin \theta ) \\ & =\text{B}(\omega \text{r})(\text{dl}\sin \theta ) \\ & =\text{B}\omega \text{rdr}\quad \{\text{dl}\sin \theta =\text{dr}\} \\ \end{align}$

∴ emf induced between any points ${{r}_{i}}$ and ${{r}_{f}}$

$=\int_{{{r}_{1}}}^{{{r}_{f}}}{B}\,\,\omega rdr=\frac{B\omega }{2}\left[ r_{f}^{2}-r_{1}^{2} \right]$

Thus to find the emf induced between any points of any shape of conductor rotating about an axis.

$\underset{(i \to \,\,f)}{\mathop{\Delta V}}\,=\frac{B\omega }{2}\left[ r_{f}^{2}-r_{i}^{2} \right]$

Direction from $\left( {{r}_{i}}\to {{r}_{f}} \right.)$ ) when $\vec{B}$ is directed $O$ (and $\omega $ is clockwise)

concepts.title: Induced Current in Closed Circuit
concepts.conceptBody:
Induced Current in Closed Circuit

In the figure shown, we can replace the moving rod ab by a battery of emf Bvl with the positive terminal at a and the negative terminal at $b$. The resistance $r$ of the rod ab may be treated as the internal resistance of the battery. Hence, the current in the circuit is,

$\text{i}=\,\,\frac{\text{e}}{\text{R}+\text{r}}$

or $I=\frac{Bvl}{R+r}$

Induction and energy transfers : In the figure shown, if you move the conductor ab with a constant velocity $v$, the current in the circuit is,

$i=\frac{Bvl}{R}\quad \,\,\,(r=0)$

 

A magnetic force ${{F}_{m}}=ilB=\frac{{{B}^{2}}{{l}^{2}}v}{R}$ acts on the conductor in opposite direction of velocity. So, to move the conductor with a constant velocity $v$ an equal and opposite force $F$ has to be applied in the conductor. Thus,

$\text{F }\,={{\text{F}}_{\text{m}}}\,\,=\frac{{{\text{B}}^{2}}{{\text{l}}^{2}}\text{v}}{\text{R}}$

The rate at which work is done by the applied force is, ${{P}_{applied }}=Fv=\frac{{{B}^{2}}{{l}^{2}}{{v}^{2}}}{R}$ and the rate at which energy is dissipated in the circuit is,

 

\[{{P}_{dissipated }}={{i}^{2}}R={{\left( \frac{Bvl}{2} \right)}^{2}}R=\frac{{{B}^{2}}{{I}^{2}}{{v}^{2}}}{R}\]

This is just equal to the rate at which work is done by the applied force.

concepts.title: Time Varying Magnetic Field
concepts.conceptBody:
Time-Varying Magnetic field  

  • Due to a time varying magnetic field, a non conservative electric field is induced inside and outside the region of magnetic field, which forms always a closed loop.
  • The electric field lines of a non conservative field is always in the form of closed loop.

Consider a cylindrical region which extends from $+\,\,\infty $ to $+\,\,\infty $ and in which the magnetic field varies as a function of time.

  1. \[For\,\,r\le R\]

consider a circular region at an axial distance r

 

From Faraday's Law, potential energy in the loop \[=\,\,\,\frac{-\,\,d\phi }{dt}\]

\[\Rightarrow \quad \,\int{\overrightarrow{E}}\cdot \overrightarrow{dl}=\frac{-d}{dt}(\overrightarrow{B}\cdot \overrightarrow{A})\]

$\Rightarrow \,\,\,\int{\overrightarrow{E}}\cdot \overrightarrow{dl}=\frac{-d}{dt}\left( B\left( -\pi {{r}^{2}} \right) \right)\,\,=\,\,\left( \frac{\text{dB}}{\text{dt}} \right)\pi {{r}^{2}}$

$\text{E}\,\,(2\pi \text{r})\,\,=\,\,\left( \frac{\text{dB}}{\text{dt}} \right)\,\,\pi {{\text{r}}^{2}}$

$\therefore \quad \,$ $r\le R$

If this is +ve, then direction of electric field is in the direction of circulation. 

2.  $for\,\,\,r \,\ge \,R$

$\int{\overrightarrow{E}}\cdot \overrightarrow{dl}=\frac{-d}{dt}\left( B\left( -\pi {{\text{R}}^{2}} \right) \right)$

$\,\therefore \quad \,\,\,\,\,\,\,E=\left( \frac{dB}{dt} \right)\left( \frac{{{R}^{2}}}{2r} \right)$

concepts.title: Linear Conductor Placed in Time Varying Magnetic Field
concepts.conceptBody:
Linear Conductor Placed in Time Varying Magnetic Field 

1.$r\le R$

 

Emf induced in the conductor$=\,\,\int{\overrightarrow{E}}\cdot \overrightarrow{dl}$

$=\int{\text{E}}\,\,dx cos\,\,\theta $

$=\,\,\int{\frac{1}{2}}\left( \frac{dB}{dt} \right)\text{r dx}\,\,\cos \,\,\theta $

$\{d \,=r\,\,cos\,\,\theta \}$ $$

2.$r\,\,>\,\,R$

Emf induced in the conductor

\[=\,\,\,\int{\text{E}\,\,dx}\,\,cos\,\,\theta \]

 

$=\int{\frac{1}{2}}\left( \frac{dB}{dt} \right)\frac{{{R}^{2}}}{r}\,\,dx\left( \frac{d}{r} \right)$

$=\frac{1}{2}\left( \frac{dB}{dt} \right){{R}^{2}}~d\,\, \int_{\text{a}}^{\text{a}+\text{l}}{\frac{dx}{\left( {{d}^{2}}+{{x}^{2}} \right)}}$

$=\frac{1}{2}\left( \frac{dB}{dt} \right){{R}^{2}}~ \,d\left| \frac{1}{~d}ta{{n}^{-1}} \right|\frac{x}{d}\|_{a}^{a+l}$

$=\frac{1}{2}\left( \frac{dB}{dt} \right){{R}^{2}}\,\,\left[ ta{{n}^{-1}}\left( \frac{a+1}{~d} \right) -ta{{n}^{-1}}\left( \frac{a}{d} \right) \right]$

\[\]
